Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model can maintain a consistent standing configuration with bounded joint motion and physically plausible contact behavior over short durations, validating the mechanical model and control structure. The presented framework emphasizes mechanical realism, structured control design, and reproducible simulation setup, providing a baseline platform for future extensions toward dynamic locomotion control.

title: Are Pose Estimators Ready for the Open World? STAGE: A GenAI Toolkit for Auditing 3D Human Pose Estimators
authors: Nikita Kister, István Sárándi, Jiayi Wang, Anna Khoreva, Gerard Pons-Moll
abstract: For safety-critical applications, it is crucial to audit 3D human pose estimators before deployment. Will the system break down if the weather or the clothing changes? Is it robust regarding gender and age? To answer these questions and more, we need controlled studies with images that differ in a single attribute, but real benchmarks cannot provide such pairs. We thus present STAGE, a GenAI data toolkit for auditing 3D human pose estimators. For STAGE, we develop the first GenAI image creator with accurate 3D pose control and propose a novel evaluation strategy to isolate and quantify the effects of single factors such as gender, ethnicity, age, clothing, location, and weather. Enabled by STAGE, we generate a series of benchmarks to audit, for the first time, the sensitivity of popular pose estimators towards such factors. Our results show that natural variations can severely degrade pose estimator performance, raising doubts about their readiness for open-world deployment. We aim to highlight these robustness issues and establish STAGE as a benchmark to quantify them.

title: Learning High-Fidelity Garment Deformation via Skinning-Free Image Transfer
authors: Rong Wang, Wei Mao, Changsheng Lu, Hongdong Li
abstract: We present a novel method for generating 3D garment deformations from underlying body poses, which is key to a wide range of applications, including virtual try-on and extended reality. To simplify the cloth dynamics, existing methods mostly rely on linear blend skinning to obtain low-frequency posed garment shape and only regress highfrequency wrinkles. However, due to the lack of explicit skinning supervision, such skinning-based approach often produces misaligned shapes when posing the garment, consequently corrupts the high-frequency signals and fails to recover high-fidelity wrinkles. To tackle this issue, we propose a skinning-free approach by independently estimating posed (i) vertex position for low-frequency posed garment shape, and (ii) vertex normal for high-frequency local wrinkle details. In this way, each frequency modality can be effectively decoupled and directly supervised by the geometry of the deformed garment. To further improve the visual quality of deformation, we propose to encode both vertex attributes as rendered texture images, so that 3D garment deformation can be equivalently achieved via 2D image transfer. This enables us to leverage powerful pretrained image models to recover fine-grained visual details in wrinkles, while maintaining superior scalability for garments of diverse topologies without relying on manual UV partition. Finally, we propose a multimodal fusion to incorporate constraints from both frequency modalities and robustly recover deformed 3D garments from transferred images. Extensive experiments show that our method significantly improves animation quality on various garment types and recovers finer wrinkles than state-of-the-art methods.

title: PASTA: Controllable Part-Aware Shape Generation with Autoregressive Transformers
authors: Songlin Li, Despoina Paschalidou, Leonidas Guibas
abstract: The increased demand for tools that automate the 3D content creation process led to tremendous progress in deep generative models that can generate diverse 3D objects of high fidelity. In this paper, we present PASTA, an autoregressive transformer architecture for generating high quality 3D shapes. PASTA comprises two main components: An autoregressive transformer that generates objects as a sequence of cuboidal primitives and a blending network, implemented with a transformer decoder that composes the sequences of cuboids and synthesizes high quality meshes for each object. Our model is trained in two stages: First we train our autoregressive generative model using only annotated cuboidal parts as supervision and next, we train our blending network using explicit 3D supervision, in the form of watertight meshes. Evaluations on various ShapeNet objects showcase the ability of our model to perform shape generation from diverse inputs e.g. from scratch, from a partial object, from text and images, as well size-guided generation, by explicitly conditioning on a bounding box that defines the object's boundaries. Moreover, as our model considers the underlying part-based structure of a 3D object, we are able to select a specific part and produce shapes with meaningful variations of this part. As evidenced by our experiments, our model generates 3D shapes that are both more realistic and diverse than existing part-based and non part-based methods, while at the same time is simpler to implement and train.

title: Implicit Preference Learning for Adaptive Human-Robot Collaboration Using Task Graphs
authors: Liliana Antão, João Reis, Gil Gonçalves
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: As human-robot collaboration (HRC) becomes increasingly essential in industrial settings, ensuring flexible and adaptive interaction is critical for enhancing productivity and worker safety. This paper presents a framework designed to enable true HRC by using task graphs, real-time human motion prediction, and online preference learning to adapt robot actions based on operator preferences and behaviors. The framework was implemented in a car axle assembly task. The system dynamically adjusted its behavior using task graphs that represented the task as granular operations such as grabbing, placing, and dropping. Human movements were predicted and actions classified using an LSTM network to anticipate and learn operator preferences within the task graph. Additionally, voice control allowed operators to command the robot during critical actions such as dropping heavy components. Experimental results with six operators demonstrated that the system achieved high adaptability, with workflow interruptions reducing by 77.6% over 15 trials and task completion time improving by 27.5%. The ability to adapt in real-time to individual operator preferences represents a significant improvement over traditional fixed-sequence workflows.

title: Corrections to “Semi-Classical Monte Carlo Simulation of Quantum Confinement Effects in Si Nanosheet and Fin FETs in Industry Standard Orientation”
authors: Uiho Lee, Jaeyeon Kim, Byoung Hak Hong, Wookhyun Kwon, Branch T. Archer, Leonard F. Register, Sanjay K. Banerjee, Jiwon Chang
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (“Semi-Classical Monte Carlo Simulation of Quantum Confinement Effects in Si Nanosheet and Fin FETs in Industry Standard Orientation”).

title: Knowledge-Driven Memetic Algorithm with Forgetting Mechanism for the Energy-Aware Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling Problem with Variable Machining Speeds
authors: Hongquan Qu, Peng Shi, Maolin Cai, Yibo Li, Xiaomeng Tong
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Against the backdrop of global energy conservation, emission reduction, and sustainable development, energy-aware scheduling in manufacturing workshops has become a growing focus. However, existing research on the energy-aware flexible job-shop scheduling problem (EFJSP) with variable machining speeds primarily considers energy consumption at the job level, neglecting the energy heterogeneity of individual operations under different constraints, a factor of key environmental significance. To address this problem, this paper innovatively incorporates processing quality constraints to define upper limits on variable speeds. The study develops a mathematical model for the speed-adjustable flexible job shop scheduling problem considering operation-level energy consumption. Considering limitations in current multi-neighborhood structure selection, we propose a knowledge-driven memetic algorithm with a forgetting mechanism (KDMA-FM). The model's effectiveness is validated through comparisons with the exact solver OR-Tools on a simplified system. Furthermore, KDMA-FM's performance is evaluated against existing algorithms across instances of varying scales. Results demonstrate that KDMA-FM outperforms benchmark algorithms in over 80% of instances and achieves notable energy savings.

title: CAOA - Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment
authors: Hiranya Garbha Kumar, Minhas Kamal, Balakrishnan Prabhakaran
abstract: Accurately aligning CAD models to their corresponding objects in indoor RGB-D scans is a central challenge in 3D semantic reconstruction. The task requires estimating a 9-Degree-of-Freedom (DoF) pose-position, rotation, and scale along three axes-but is hindered by noisy and incomplete scans, as well as segmentation errors that cause geometric distortions. We present Completion-Assisted Ob-ject-CAD Alignment (CAOA), a method that integrates a semantically and contextually aware point cloud completion module with a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm, enabling precise alignment of CAD models to scanned objects. Existing completion methods are typically trained and evaluated on synthetic datasets, which often fail to generalize to real-world scans. To bridge this gap, we introduce a synthetic data generation strategy tailored to indoor scenes, significantly reducing the synthetic-to-real domain gap-validated through quantitative comparisons with widely used completion datasets. In addition, we release S 2 C-Completion, an expert-annotated dataset of over 8,500 object-CAD pairs from Scan2CAD, created for real-world indoor single-object completion and intended as a new benchmark for this task. For object-CAD alignment, we incorporate symmetry information via a symmetry-aware loss, improving robustness to symmetric ambiguities. On the Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17 % accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods. All code, datasets, and annotation tools will be publicly available on GitHub.

title: Multi-source Fusion Positioning Revisited by Drawing on Human Thinking Process
authors: Jianfan Chen, Qingquan Li, Ke Dong, Jianghai Liao, Dejin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Reliable and long-term positioning is a fundamental requirement for deploying robots across various real-world scenes. Although multi-source fusion has been widely used to enhance positioning robustness, most existing methods are designed for specific scenes and lack generalizability. This reveals that the limitations of current approaches do not stem from a lack of technical tools, but rather from the robot's inability to adaptively select suitable positioning strategies under different spatiotemporal conditions. Inspired by human thinking process—such as information filtering, memory reliability evaluation, and knowledge-based reasoning when faced with multiple potential positioning cues—this paper systematically rethinks the design of multi-source fusion positioning. We propose a novel framework with self-assessment capabilities, aiming to make real-time decisions on the optimal source combination scheme to provide continuous and robust positioning results. Experimental results further verify the robustness of the proposed method under environmental uncertainty, demonstrating its potential in unifying the source selection process in multi-source fusion positioning.

title: Multi-Module Cooperative Triggering Strategy for Pulse Power Supply Based on Online Prediction
authors: Xin Su, Tao Ma, Xiao Zhang, Yufeng Dai, Junyong Lu
abstract: This article proposes an online prediction-based cooperative triggering strategy with dynamic adjustment to address the challenges of current waveform control in multi-module pulse power supply (PPS) systems. A nonlinear system model incorporating time-varying load impedance is established. This model enables the real-time prediction of current transients based on active current feedback and the dynamic adjustment of discharge timing for each pulse-forming unit (PFU). This approach achieves the precise synthesis of a flat-top current waveform under highly nonlinear and time-varying load conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared to the offline iterative calculation method, the proposed method significantly reduces current ripple while maintaining a constant target muzzle velocity. Specifically, it reduces the peak overload by 12.6% and the peak jerk (the time derivative of acceleration) by 13.6%. Consequently, this study provides a practical and efficient solution for achieving high-precision, low-impulse control in modern PPS applications.

title: Large-Scale and High-Sensitivity Quasi-Distributed Vibration Sensing Based on Time-Division Multiplexed Dual CFBGs
authors: Xin Gui, Zhe Wang, Changjia Wang, Yiming Wang, Jiaqi Wang, Zhengying Li
abstract: To meet the technical requirements for high sensitivity quasi-distributed vibration sensing in fields such as structural health monitoring (SHM), energy exploration, and underwater acoustic surveillance, this study proposes an innovative quasi-distributed point-like vibration sensing scheme based on dual chirped fiber Bragg gratings (CFBGs). By utilizing pairs of CFBGs with a 20-mm spacing as point-like sensing units and leveraging the low temporal coherence of the Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE) source, the proposed scheme effectively avoids self-interference of the reflected light without altering the optical pulse modulation width. Incorporating path matched interferometry, the system realizes high-sensitivity phase detection for single-point vibration, achieving a sensitivity of 33.82 dB re rad/g and an acceleration resolution of 11.4 μg/√Hz at 100Hz. By combining ultra-short gauge length sensing with time-division multiplexing (TDM), the system successfully realizes the large-scale multiplexing of 240 sensing units at a sampling rate of 10 kHz. This scheme overcomes the technical bottleneck of traditional methods in balancing high sensitivity with large-scale multiplexing capacity in quasi-distributed vibration detection, providing a novel solution for applications requiring large-scale, high-sensitivity, and precise point-based vibration monitoring.

title: High-Isolation Free-Space Optical Circulator for High-Capacity Bidirectional Satellite Links
authors: Takashi Kan, Kensuke Murakami, Akihiko Nishitani, Akira Akasaka, Shota Ishimura, Tomonori Yazaki, Shin Fukuhara, Hidenori Takahashi, Takehiro Tsuritani
abstract: High-capacity satellite free-space optical (FSO) communication systems require effective transmit–receive (Tx Rx) separation to enable bidirectional operation while handling large power disparities between Tx and Rx signals. Conventional Tx–Rx separation schemes based on spatial, polarization, wavelength, or temporal domains provide high isolation but inherently limit achievable capacity by consuming degrees of freedom that could otherwise be used for signal multiplexing. Optical circulators (CIRs) represent a promising alternative; however, the Tx–Rx isolation of existing devices is insufficient for satellite FSO applications. In this paper, we present the development of a high-isolation free-space optical circulator (FSO CIR) achieving 90 dB isolation over the 1535–1567 nm wavelength range. We show that Tx–Rx crosstalk (XT) in conventional CIRs originates from back-reflected light generated by internal reflection and scattering, and we provide a quantitative evaluation of this back-reflected light based on both theoretical analysis and experimental measurements. By systematically suppressing these reflections, the proposed FSO-CIR achieves significantly enhanced isolation. Furthermore, we demonstrate a 1-Tb/s bidirectional optical communication experiment using the proposed FSO-CIR and show that the improved isolation enhances demodulation performance in the forward direction under bidirectional operation compared with a conventional CIR. These results indicate the potential of high-isolation FSO-CIRs for future satellite FSO communication systems.

title: 5GLoc: Graph-Based Learning for Robust 5G Indoor Localization under Dynamic Anchor Visibility
authors: Teng Wang, Guoliang Chen, Jingxue Bi, Jiaqi Dong, Zitao Lin, Meng Sun, Jie Liu
abstract: With the evolution of Fifth-Generation (5G) wireless systems, high-resolution channel observations enabled by large bandwidths and massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) have opened new opportunities for indoor positioning. However, while existing fingerprinting-based learning methods excel in complex multipath environments, they often rely on fixed anchor configurations. This makes them vulnerable to dynamic anchor visibility caused by signal blockages, user mobility, or hardware failures in practical deployments. To address this, we propose 5G Graph-based Localization (5GLoc), a learning-driven framework designed for robust indoor positioning under dynamic anchor availability. By modeling localization instances as graphs, 5GLoc utilizes a Dynamic Graph Attention Network (DGAT) to flexibly accommodate varying numbers and combinations of anchors, ensuring robust inference under heterogeneous observations. We further introduce a Combinatorial Dynamic Node Masking (CDNM) strategy for data augmentation, as well as a composite loss function with learnable weights for balancing accuracy and stability. Finally, a map-constrained Particle Filter (PF) is integrated to refine trajectories for spatial feasibility and trajectory consistency. Experimental results based on the IPIN 2025 dataset demonstrate that 5GLoc achieves superior performance in complex warehouse environments, with a 75th percentile error of 0.857 m. Compared to several representative positioning methods, 5GLoc reduces the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 42.2%–80.2% and 50.6%–80.8%, respectively, maintaining high reliability even under extreme conditions with reduced anchor availability. This framework provides an effective solution for 5G localization in dynamic environments, meeting both precision and real-time requirements.

title: DefectSynth: Few-Shot Defective Image Generation by Modeling Shape and Appearance
authors: Dexu Zhao, Xukun Qin, Xinghui Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Since the acquisition of a large amount of defect data is expensive and time-consuming, the limited availability of defective samples impairs the accuracy and generalizability of defect detection methods. Although defect generation approaches have been explored for data augmentation, they usually suffer from either a lack of realism or limited diversity. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage controllable few-shot<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> defective image generation network, namely, DefectSynth<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which models both the shape and appearance of defects. The first stage aims to generate continuous defect masks even with a few real masks. To this end, we propose a Hybrid Mask Interpolation (HMI) module, which performs interpolation in the image or latent space. The second stage is used to synthesize the defective appearance. We first fine-tune the pre-trained ControlNet, which is then used together with a pre-trained stable diffusion model to synthesize defective images. Given a mask generated in the first stage and a text prompt, they are integrated with a defect-free image to synthesize a high-fidelity defective image. To alleviate the issue of generation of indistinct defects with existing methods, we propose a Selective Attention Enhancement (SAE) mechanism that highlights the details of defects. We also design a Similarity-Based Feature Fusion (SFF) module to merge different local features, thereby further enriching the appearance diversity of defects. Using the defect data generated by DefectSynth, the classification accuracy on MVTec-AD has been improved from 44.03% to 66.70% compared with the baseline without synthetic data augmentation, while the F1-Score values computed on GDXray and DeepCrack for small-defect localization have been increased from 70.48% to 76.64% and from 70.08% to 83.27%, respectively. These performance gains should be due to the fact that our method is able to generate realistic and diverse defective images by modeling both the shape and appearance of defects.

title: Cinematic Memories: EEG-Based Decoding of Recall Using Naturalistic Stimuli
authors: Ana Matran-Fernandez, Sebastian Halder
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Identifying neural correlates of long-term memory retrieval has been an area of considerable research. Previous studies used custom-built, static stimuli to elicit and study memory processes, limiting the insights into brain activity in a natural environment. Instead, we used movie clips to identify neural correlates of memory retrieval. We extracted features from the time-frequency domain from electroencephalography (EEG) signals to classify whether a 10-second-long video clip had been remembered, obtaining greater-than-chance memory decoding (median area under the receiver operating characteristic curve = 0.64) in a naturalistic movie-watching task with greater real-world validity than word-list or image recall paradigms. Feature analysis revealed that remembered clips, compared to not recognised clips, showed increased event-related desynchronisation (ERD) in the theta and low alpha bands early after stimulus onset, followed by stronger ERD in the alpha bands. While beta-band differences were observed at the group level, high within-class variability precluded their use for reliable single-trial classification, with theta and alpha activity emerging as the primary discriminative features. Our ERD/S analysis generalises observations previously demonstrated in the literature using simpler stimuli and more controlled experimental designs. Finally, we observed a significant correlation between classifier performance and retrospective memory ratings of participants (ρ = 0.49) demonstrating that individual differences in memory ability are reflected in neural activity patterns. This work demonstrates the potential of naturalistic stimuli to advance memory research using EEG, revealing neural dynamics that may not be detectable with simpler stimuli, with possible implications for the development of clinical tools in memory assessment.

title: Consumer Electronics in Healthcare and Medicine: a report from the EMBS Young Professional Roundtable
authors: Declan O’Loughlin, Ilaria Facchi, Neelesh Kumar, Patrycja Lebiecka-Johansen
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Wearable consumer electronics such as smart watches, rings and hearables are increasingly used to collect real-time physiological and activity data for health monitoring and disease prevention. As researchers and practitioners at the forefront of wearable technologies in health and medicine, the Young Professionals of EMBS organized a Roundtable Discussion on the 17<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> of July 2025 at the 47<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC’25) to identify key challenges and recommendations for the field and we would like to highlight some key recommendations and insights from our discussions for the broader community.

title: Globally Consistent RGB-D SLAM with 2D Gaussian Splatting_supp1-3697159.mp4
authors: Xingguang Zhong
abstract: Recently, 3D Gaussian splatting-based RGB-D SLAM displays remarkable performance of high-fidelity 3D reconstruction. However, 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) suffers from a lack of depth rendering consistency, which leads to suboptimal geometric reconstruction in 3DGS-based SLAM. In addition, 3DGS-based SLAM methods typically lack efficient loop closure, limiting their ability to build globally consistent maps online. In this paper, we present 2DGS-SLAM, an RGB-D SLAM system using 2D Gaussian splatting as map representation. By leveraging the depth-consistent rendering property of the 2D variant, we propose an accurate camera pose optimization method and achieve geometrically accurate 3D reconstruction. In addition, we implement efficient loop detection and camera relocalization by leveraging MASt3R, a feed-forward 3D reconstruction model, and achieve efficient map updates by maintaining a local active map. Experiments show that our 2DGS-SLAM approach achieves superior tracking accuracy, higher surface reconstruction quality, and more consistent global map reconstruction compared to existing rendering-based SLAM methods, while maintaining high-fidelity image rendering and improved computational efficiency.

title: Dual-Band Rectifying Circuit With Quasi-Independent Frequency Tunability Using a Lumped-Element T-Section Network
authors: Shaopeng Wan, Zhelin Tang, Bin He, Ping Lu, Xiaoyan Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a dual-band rectifying circuit employing a T-section matching network with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$LC$</tex-math> </inline-formula> elements. The proposed matching network comprises a capacitor and two inductors to achieve impedance matching at their frequency bands for quasi-independent tuning capability, where each inductor is designed to independently tune the resonant frequency in one band while exerting negligible influence on the other frequency band. For demonstration, a dual-band rectifying circuit working at 0.9 and 1.8 GHz is designed using a lumped-element T-section matching network. The measured results show that the power conversion efficiency (PCE) reached 69.2% and 60.2% at their respective operating frequencies under 0 dBm input power. The proposed dual-band rectifying circuit has the merits of quasi-independent frequency tunability, high conversion efficiency, and compact size.

title: Rank-Based Ensemble Learning for Early Detection of Mental Health Disorders in Private University Students
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo, K.M. Arafat Islam, Md. Sazzadur Ahamed, Md. Fokhray Hossain, Maimun Nahar Rahi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Stress, anxiety and depression are particularly becoming common concerns in the private universities which were demanding for more academia, had greater financial pressure and stiff societal pressures, all tend to escalate psychological tension among students. The objective of this study is to introduce a behavioral analysis framework using machine learning for the early detection of mental health problems in private university students in Bangladesh. Information was collected using clinically validated questionnaire studying behavioral patterns including sleep quality, study habits and digital activity. The 4 baseline models, SAINT (Self-Attention and Intersample Network), TabNet, NODE (Neural Oblivious Decision Ensemble), and CatBoost were trained without fine-grained hyperparameter tuning to look at the discrimination ability of the predictive model. To stabilize and generalize, we also built a Rank-Based Ensemble Model with CatBoost, SAINT and NODE. CatBoost presented the inappropriately high individual accuracy above all models (the maximum accuracy 99.55%), and maintained robust performance of 98% even after integrating all predictions, which is a result with no less precision nor recall for any disorders. Results suggest that behavioral data, even when used by untuned machine learning models, can outperform chance in predicting levels of stress-, anxiety- and depressionrelated disorders (Low, Moderate, Severe) of private state university students and open the doors to scalable and interpretable early mental health detection systems at academic institutions.

title: Hybrid Transfer Learning with Dual-Stage Metaheuristic Optimization: A Comparative Study on Regional Energy Forecasting
authors: Mehrab Hussain Zuhan, Al Hamza, Abdul Baki Shahed, Md. Milon Khan, Mirza Md. Mahbubur Rahman, Nusaiba Binte Habib
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate short-term load forecasting is critical for the stability and efficiency of modern power systems. However, conventional statistical and deep learning models often underperform when trained on small or region-specific datasets due to limited generalization and overfitting. This study introduces a hybrid forecasting framework that integrates transfer learning (TL), online learning, and a dual-stage Grid Search–Particle Swarm Optimization (GS–PSO) strategy to enhance prediction accuracy and adaptability under data-scarce conditions. The proposed model leverages a pretrained three-layer LSTM network trained on the large-scale Australia East Power (AEP) dataset and fine-tunes it for the Sylhet regional grid in Bangladesh. In the first stage, Grid Search establishes stable architectural parameters, while PSO subsequently optimizes performance-sensitive hyperparameters, including learning rates, dropout, and bias correction factors. The system further employs an online learning mechanism to adapt dynamically to real-time variations in demand. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed Transfer Learning LSTM with GS–PSO achieves superior performance (MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=15.76$</tex>, RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=20.67$</tex>, MAPE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=1.43 \%$</tex>, and Accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=98.57 \%$</tex>), outperforming conventional LSTM and non-PSO baselines, in the other hand Offline transfer learning LSTM with PSO has achieved a mentionable accuracy of 97.76%. The findings highlight the potential of combining transfer learning with metaheuristic optimization to build continuously adaptive and computationally efficient forecasting systems for regional energy management.

title: Interpretable Plant Leaf Recognition: Calibration and Domain Shift Analysis With Occlusion Sensitivity and Grad-Cam
authors: Gazi Tahsina Sharmin Jahin, Firoze Maliha, M. Jamshed Alam Patwary
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate plant recognition is essential in agriculture, biodiversity monitoring, and decision-making, but most research focuses on accuracy rather than robustness, calibration, and explainability. This study presents a comprehensive framework for Egyptian plant leaf classification using 3,588 images across eight species. Six CNN architectures were utilized, with InceptionV3 achieving the highest accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(98.5 \%)$</tex>, followed by DenseNet121 (98.2 %) and EfficientNetB3 (97.0 %). Besides accuracy, we evaluated robustness, calibration, interpretability, and sensitivity to domain shift. Results showed resistance to blur but vulnerability to noise and lighting variation. InceptionV3 was accurate and well-calibrated(ECE 4.6 %). Occlusion and Grad-CAM confirmed biologically significant features. The novelty lies in an end-to-end evaluation pipeline that yields insights for reliable agricultural deployment.

title: A Data-Driven Approach to Sustainable Agriculture: Integrated IoT, Machine Learning, and Automation in Smart Greenhouses
authors: Atiqur Rahman, Md. Moniruzzaman Hemal, Md. Naeem Islam, Sadia Hossain, Md. Maruf Murshed, Md. Rashidul Islam, Md. Toukir Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Meeting rising food demand sustainably requires production systems that increase yield per unit of water and energy while limiting environmental impact. We develop and evaluate an integrated controlled-environment agriculture platform that unifies horizontal greenhouse beds, vertical racks, and hydroponic channels with real-time sensing of temperature, humidity, light, and moisture; closed-loop climate and irrigation control; and a machine-learning crop recommender. An ESP32 telemetry layer streams data to the cloud for monitoring and analysis, while a dual-axis solar-tracking photovoltaic subsystem and automated rainwater harvesting reduce dependence on the grid and freshwater extraction. Experiments with a functional prototype deployed in Gazipur, Bangladesh, demonstrate stable closed-loop operation. The solar tracking system improved energy harvesting efficiency by approximately 18 % compared to static panels, while the rainwater system successfully captured precipitation to offset irrigation needs. The boosting ensemble achieved 97 % across accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and MCC, macro averaged using a one-vs-rest protocol. Detailed analysis reveals that while the model generalizes well, specific legume classes exhibit minor confusion due to similar nutrient profiles. These results show that tightly integrating perception, learning, and actuation within a resource-aware greenhouse can deliver high reliability and sustainability. Future work will expand local data collection to bridge the domain gap between public datasets and local sensor noise.

title: Exploring the Effectiveness of ViT_B16 for Early Breast Cancer Diagnosis in Mammogram Mastery
authors: Effat Ara Naznin Ema, Md Jahangir Alam Khondkar, Md Samsuzzaman, Md. Abdul Masud, Md Mohidul Alam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Breast cancer is a leading cause of death worldwide, particularly among women, making the development of precise and efficient detection techniques crucial. Diagnostics from conventional mammography screening may be delayed or misleading because of human error and variability. Improving diagnostic accuracy has been demonstrated to be possible with deep learning models, particularly transformer-based architectures. In this study, we fine-tuned a Vision Transformer (ViT_B16) model pre-trained on ImageNet for automated breast cancer detection using X-ray images. The classification head of ViT_B16 was adapted for binary classification (Cancer vs. Non-Cancer). With an accuracy of 98.67 %, precision of 1.0, recall (sensitivity) of 92.3 %, specificity of 1.0, F1-score of 0.96, and an AUC-ROC of 1.0 on the held-out test set, the fine-tuned ViT_B16 demonstrated strong performance. These results show that transformer-based models with excellent sensitivity and specificity, such ViT B16, are promising models for identifying breast cancer in mammography. These findings indicate that while Vision Transformers do not yet outperform strong CNN models on small datasets, they may be a helpful tool for mammography analysis.

title: Synthesis and Characterization of Ni-Doped $\text{SnO}_{2}$ Nanoparticles Prepared by Sol-Gel Method for Photocatalytic Treatment of Textile Wastewater
authors: Rezwanus Sadat, Md. Faruk Hossain, Sakib Hossen, Sanjeeda Islam Mou, Rudro Karmokar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Tin oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SnO}_{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>) is an n-type semiconductor known for its stability, transparency, and photocatalytic activity. In this work, nickel (Ni) has been introduced as a dopant to modify the structural and optical characteristics of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SnO}_{2}$</tex>. Ni-doped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SnO}_{2}$</tex> nanoparticles are synthesized by the sol-gel method, and thin films are fabricated through spin coating on glass substrates followed by annealing at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$500{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>. Structural and optical characterizations have been performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), and UV-Visible spectroscopy. The results confirm that Ni has successfully incorporated into the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SnO}_{2}$</tex> lattice, which led to a reduction in crystallite size and a gradual narrowing of the optical band gap as Ni doping level increased up to 15%. Although higher levels of doping cause a slight decrease in photocatalytic efficiency during dye degradation, the enhanced film uniformity and tunable band structure point to significant potential for thin-film, sensor, and photocatalytic applications. This work represents that transition-metal doping is an effective way to tailor the physicochemical properties of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SnO}_{2}$</tex> for a variety of energy and environmental applications.

title: Towards High-Performance Tandem Silicon Perovskite Photovoltaic Cell: OghmaNano Simulation
authors: Rubayat Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Photovoltaic energy is one of the most advanced fields of research in the world, and it is a significant part of clean energy. The main challenge of this photovoltaic technology is its limited efficiency of power conversion. As per the Shockley-Queisser limit, the efficiency of power conversion of a single junction photovoltaic cell cannot surpass 33.7%. Multi-junction photovoltaic cells, like a tandem silicon perovskite photovoltaic cell, are reaching higher power conversion efficiency in recent studies. This study uses several layer configurations to investigate the performance of a tandem silicon perovskite photovoltaic cell model. Using a typical tandem silicon perovskite photovoltaic cell configuration, the efficiency of power conversion without a hole transport layer was determined to be 32.214%. For the silicon layer, seven different thicknesses were employed. The usage of a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}$</tex> thick silicon layer and an absorber layer of perovskite <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 10^{-7} ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> has demonstrated the highest efficiency of 36.29%.

title: SafeBirth AI: Tuned Explainable Ensembles for Maternal and Fetal Health Classification
authors: Mamunur Rashid Alex, Md Redwan Hossain, Sadia Afroz, Refat - E Sabnur, Najmus Saadat
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Maternal and fetal health problems remain a serious global issue, where early and accurate risk assessment during pregnancy is essential for safe childbirth. Even though machine learning is capable of assisting clinicians by outperforming simple interpretation of cardiotocography signals and clinical measurements, the issues of class imbalance, unstable validation, and not clearly-defined model decisions remain as obstacles to the practice. To address these issues, Proposee SafeBirth AI framework based on an ensemble of classifiers: Random Forest (RF), XGBoost (XGB), Decision Tree (DT). After data cleaning and scaling, a hybrid SMOTE + Random OverSampling (ROS) strategy is used to balance the classes, and Bayesian optimization together with GridSearch is applied for hyperparameter tuning. Experiments on the CTG Fetal Health dataset (2,126 records) and the Maternal Health Risk dataset (1,000 records) show that SafeBirth AI reaches 98.39% and 98.21% accuracy for hard and soft voting on CTG, and 98% accuracy on the Maternal dataset, with strong F1-scores under stratified cross-validation. Moreover, SHAP and LIME accentuate the most significant aspects and describe single predictions, which contribute to clear and reliable monitoring of maternal and fetal health.

title: Automatic Stress Detection From Code-Mixed Tamil-English Social Media Posts Using Deep Learning Models
authors: Rowshon Akter, Md Ashraful Alam, Mohammed Moshiul Hoque
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Stress is a significant issue affecting people across professions, lifestyles, and age groups. The rise of social media platforms such as Twitter, WhatsApp, and Facebook, where people frequently share their daily activities and moods, provides valuable data for stress detection. However, past research has predominantly targeted high-resource languages like English, overlooking low-resource languages such as Tamil. The unique linguistic intricacies of Tamil, combined with a lack of comprehensive resources and tools, pose distinct challenges for this study. This paper offers an intelligent approach to identifying stress in Tamil codemixed social media posts and explores various machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and transformerbased techniques. Experimental results indicate that the BiLSTM model outperforms other baseline models, achieving the highest macro <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f_{1}$</tex>-score of 0.76 in the stress identification task.

title: An Analytical Framework for Integrating Artificial Intelligence Into Cybersecurity Decision-Making
authors: Afia Naeem Alam Din, Wael Elmedany
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative force in cybersecurity, reshaping both defensive strategies and offensive capacities. This study examines the dual role of AI in cybersecurity by proposing a theoretical framework that reflects AI's dual-purpose nature: its ability to protect through defensive innovation and its potential to harm through adversarial abuse. The framework combines technical, ethical, and governance perspectives, demonstrating how feedback loops, risk zones, and sectoral applications interact to influence cybersecurity decision-making. The study compared models such as Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, LightGBM, Support Vector Machine (SVM), KNN, and TensorFlow ANN across accuracy, precision, recall, and resilience, with results benchmarked against findings reported in the literature. The analysis highlighted trade-offs in efficiency and interpretability, while latency and adversarially robustness tests underscored real-world constraints. The supporting simulations establish the framework's practical significance, ensuring that it is both theoretical and practically grounded.

title: Design of an Iot-Based Smart Water Quality Monitoring System with Artificial Intelligence
authors: Hala Ben Ali, Elaf Ebdah, Luae Al-Tarawneh
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Monitoring water quality is crucial for protecting public health, aquatic ecosystems, and sustainable water resources, especially in water-scarce regions. This project proposes an intelligent, IoT-based water quality monitoring system that leverages real-time sensing, cloud computing, and machine learning. It is proposed that the system be equipped with environmental sensors to measure water quality parameters such as pH, turbidity, temperature, and total dissolved solids (TDS). An ESP32S3 processor is used to wirelessly transmit sensor data to the cloud for storage, display, and analysis. According to experimental results, the model achieved 95% accuracy and an F1 value of 0.95, making it a reliable pollution prediction model. In conclusion, the system offers an economical and environmentally friendly method for achieving real-time water quality monitoring and intelligent water resource management, so that the system can be deployed in water bodies, such as rivers, reservoirs and water distribution networks.

title: State of Charge Estimation for Electric Vehicle Batteries Using Temporal Convolutional Networks
authors: Ghada Toumi, Abdelaziz Sahbani, Hela Mahersia
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Accurate state of charge (SoC) estimation of batteries is a critical requirement for energy management systems in electric vehicles operating under real-world driving conditions. Recent deep learning approaches have shown promising performance; however, most studies rely on recurrent architectures with limited comparative evaluations. In this paper, we present a deep learning-based framework for SoC estimation based on Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) under identical experimental conditions using the IEEE Battery and Heating Data in Real Driving Cycles dataset, featuring real-world EV driving data across summer and winter scenarios. The proposed TCN model leverages dilated temporal convolutions to capture long-term dependencies in battery dynamics without relying on recurrent structures. Performance is evaluated using standard metrics, including RMSE, MAE, MSE and the coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex>). Comparative results demonstrate that the TCN-based approach in some cases, improved SoC estimation accuracy compared to the baseline deep learning model, particularly under challenging seasonal operating conditions. These findings highlight the potential of TCN architectures as an efficient and robust alternative for SoC estimation in real-world electric vehicle applications.

title: Modeling of a Vibration Isolation System for Precision Manufacturing
authors: Jixing Che, Wei Zhang, Wei Jiang, Jiulin Wu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The performance of low-frequency vibration isolators is critical to manufacturing precision, due to the reduction of harmful vibrations in precision equipment. Isolators comprise multiple components arranged in series or parallel, leading to challenges such as crosstalk and modal coupling, which complicate the dynamic behavior and degrade isolation performance. A multi-body dynamic model of a vibration isolation system is developed in this paper, by analyzing the transmission paths of vibration excitations in each degree of freedom and testing the modal characteristic of the key module. Furthermore, the parametric study based on the dynamic model is established to evaluate the effects of the design variables, thereby supporting performance prediction and optimal design of vibration isolation systems. The modal frequencies between structures and components should be elevated above the operating bandwidth, and appropriate dynamic parameters should be configured to reduce crosstalk between degrees of freedom. This study provides a theoretical and simulation-based foundation for the design and tuning of high-performance low-frequency vibration isolators.

title: Edge AI with on-Device Learning Capabilities for Precision Agriculture
authors: Nicola Rozzi, Gabriele Penzotti, Michele Amoretti, Stefano Caselli
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Efficient water management in agriculture plays a strategic role in ensuring the economic sustainability of production and, at the same time, the conservation of natural resources. The adoption of innovative monitoring and control techniques makes it possible to improve irrigation efficiency, reducing waste and ensuring that water is supplied according to the actual needs of the crops. The work reported in this paper addresses this problem by designing and prototyping an intelligent, low-power embedded system for on-device measurement and calibration of soil moisture in precision agriculture scenarios. The goal is to develop a platform composed of sensor nodes interconnected through an IoT network equipped with sensors that differ in terms of accuracy and cost, and capable of locally learning and improving measurement quality by integrating on-device learning techniques. The proposed system is based on the idea of performing on-device recalibration driven by reference values that are received intermittently or periodically. In particular, it leverages the TinyRBF algorithm, a recently proposed neural network architecture that can be executed on embedded nodes under tight energy constraints.

title: RGB-D and Wrist-Mounted IMU Sensor Integration for a Robust Human‒Robot Handover System in Surgical Environments
authors: F. F. Rojas Ávila, P. R. Moreno Andrade, A. S. Sanchez Huapaya, J. L. Becerra Felipe, A. K. Nieves Acosta, J. A. Carrizales Navarro
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The handover of surgical instruments between medical staff and robotic assistants constitutes a central challenge in human-robot interaction (HRI), where spatial accuracy, robustness to occlusions, and correct gesture interpretation are decisive for task safety and fluency. In surgical environments, the coexistence of multiple operators and overlapping hands hinders reliable detection of the handover point, limiting the effectiveness of purely visual approaches. This work proposes a sensor integration system that combines information from a low-cost RGB-D camera and an inertial measurement unit (IMU) mounted on the surgeon's wrist, with the aim of improving spatial estimation, orientation tracking, and dynamic gesture interpretation during handover. The system performs three-dimensional recognition of the surgeon's hand using machine learning techniques, fusing the detected geometry with metric depth data. In parallel, the IMU provides orientation and motion dynamics, enabling anticipation of instrument request or reception gestures and reinforcing the stability of spatial estimation. Results in a simulated environment show a reduction in root mean square error (RMSE) from 2.9 cm to 1.7 cm, equivalent to a 41 % improvement compared to purely visual methods. In addition, an 18 % increase in temporal trajectory consistency and a 23 % reduction in gesture recognition failures were observed, validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach. This system promotes progress toward safer, more adaptive, and clinically viable collaborative surgical assistants.

title: Estimating Rotation in $\mathbb{S O}(3)$ by Torus on $\mathbb{S}^{3}$
authors: Yinlong Liu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Rotation motion in the three-dimensional physical world refers to an angular displacement of an object around a specific axis in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{R}^{3}$</tex>, and it is a basic physical motion for various real-world objects. Accordingly, estimating the rotation of a target from input observations is one of the most important tasks in robotics and computer vision. However, the rotated motion is typically formulated as a non-linear and non-convex process due to the nonlinearity and nonconvexity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{S O}(3)$</tex>, which leads to the difficulty of solving rotation estimation. It is acknowledged that there are many methods for solving specific types of rotation estimation problems, and most of them are algebraic ways. On the contrast, this paper introduces a new geometrical perspective that any three-dimensional rotation that satisfies a specific motion can be represented by a torus on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{S}^{3}$</tex>, and the torus can be formulated by a constrained linear system. Based on this theory, this paper introduces a novel geometrical way to estimate rotation in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{S O}(3)$</tex> by exploring the intersections of many tori on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{S}^{3}$</tex>.

title: LaMP: Learning Robust Latent Motion Prior for Optimization-Based Human Motion Generation
authors: Xiaozhong Lyu, Korrawe Karunratanakul, Kaifeng Zhao, Siyu Tang
abstract: We present Latent Motion Prior (LaMP), a novel framework for learning a generalizable human motion prior that enables efficient optimization for a wide range of motion-related tasks, including text-to-motion generation, motion editing, motion blending, motion refinement, and environment-aware collision avoidance. LaMP employs a body part-based encoder to learn a disentangled latent representation of human motion, together with a masked training strategy that encourages the model to capture the most informative structural and dynamic aspects of the motion. As a result, LaMP produces a robust and expressive latent space that serves as a latent motion prior across diverse downstream tasks. We evaluate the learned representation on a wide range of optimization-based downstream tasks. Experimental results show that the current families of text-to-motion models are generally not suitable to serve as a motion prior, while LaMP consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art methods across all optimization tasks. The code is available at: https://github.com/lvsean/LaMP.

title: NeuSEditor: From Multi-View Images to Text-Guided Neural Surface Edits
authors: Nail Ibrahimli, Julian F. P. Kooij, Liangliang Nan
abstract: Implicit surface representations are valued for their compactness and continuity, but they pose significant challenges for editing. Despite recent advancements, existing methods often fail to preserve identity and maintain geometric consistency during editing. To address these challenges, we present NeuSEditor, a novel method for text-guided editing of neural implicit surfaces derived from multi-view images. NeuSEditor introduces an identity-preserving architecture that efficiently separates scenes into foreground and background, enabling precise modifications without altering the scene-specific elements. Our geometry-aware distillation loss significantly enhances rendering and geometric quality. Our method simplifies the editing workflow by eliminating the need for continuous dataset updates and source prompting. NeuSEditor outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods, delivering superior quantitative and qualitative results. For visual results, visit: neuseditor.github.io.

title: SCENIC: Scene-Aware Semantic Navigation with Instruction-Guided Control
authors: Xiaohan Zhang, Sebastian Starke, Vladimir Guzov, Zhensong Zhang, Eduardo Pérez-Pellitero, Gerard Pons-Moll
abstract: Synthesizing natural human motion that adapts to complex environments while allowing creative control remains a fundamental challenge in motion synthesis. Existing models often fall short, either by assuming flat terrain or lacking the ability to control motion semantics through text. To address these limitations, we introduce SCENIC, a diffusion model designed to generate human motion that adapts to dynamic terrains within virtual scenes while enabling semantic control through natural language. The key technical challenge lies in simultaneously reasoning about complex scene geometry while maintaining text control. This requires understanding both high-level navigation goals and fine-grained environmental constraints. The model must ensure physical plausibility and precise navigation across varied terrain, while also preserving user-specified text control, such as “carefully stepping over obstacles” or “walking upstairs like a zombie.” Our solution introduces a hierarchical scene reasoning approach. At the core of our method is a novel hierarchical scene reasoning framework. It combines two key components: a motion-scene cross-attention block that aligns the human body's motion features with local scene geometry, enabling precise low-level interactions; and a target point canonicalization module that provides global goal conditioning by normalizing target scene coordinates for high-level guidance. To ensure plausibility and naturalness, we leverage a pre-trained motion diffusion prior and apply scene-constrained diffusion noise optimization during sampling, enabling long-horizon motion generation that respects both scene structure and semantic text input. Experiments demonstrate that our novel diffusion model generates arbitrarily long human motions that both adapt to complex scenes with varying terrain surfaces and respond to textual prompts. Additionally, we show SCENIC can generalize to four real-scene datasets.

title: FASTMESH: Efficient Artistic Mesh Generation Via Component Decoupling
authors: Jeonghwan Kim, Yushi Lan, Armando Fortes, Yongwei Chen, Xingang Pan
abstract: Recent mesh generation approaches typically tokenize triangle meshes into sequences of tokens and train autoregressive models to generate these tokens sequentially. Despite substantial progress, such token sequences inevitably reuse vertices multiple times to fully represent manifold meshes, as each vertex is shared by multiple faces. This redundancy leads to excessively long token sequences and inefficient generation processes. In this paper, we propose an efficient framework that generates artistic meshes by treating vertices and faces separately, significantly reducing redundancy. We employ an autoregressive model solely for vertex generation, decreasing the token count to approximately 23% of that required by the most compact existing tokenizer. Next, we leverage a bidirectional transformer to complete the mesh in a single step by capturing intervertex relationships and constructing the adjacency matrix that defines the mesh faces. To further improve the generation quality, we introduce a fidelity enhancer to refine vertex positioning into more natural arrangements and propose a post-processing framework to remove undesirable edge connections. Experimental results show that our method achieves more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times$</tex> faster speed on mesh generation compared to state-of-the-art approaches, while producing higher mesh quality.

title: Continuous-Wave THz Imaging of Moisture Distribution During Ceramic Drying
authors: Emil John Y. Magaway, Aleksander M. Cendrowicz, Zhenyu J. Zhang, Miguel Navarro-Cía
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: The lack of a non-contact methodology to monitor the ceramic drying process impedes the optimization of this critical production stage. Resorting to continuous-wave THz imaging, we are able to generate images of the water dis tribution within samples and, when paired with single-point dielectric property data, produce absorption coefficient maps of two ceramic samples as a function of drying time and water mass fraction of the whole system. This highlights the spatially-dependent drying time of ceramic samples due to local variation of material properties (e.g., porosity) that cannot be captured with the contact gravimetry industry standard. The THz continuous-wave system enables contactless, spatially-resolved visualisation and tracking of moisture distribution during drying, however, for quantitative interpretation of absorption coefficients complementary THz-TDS measurements are needed to provide the dielectric properties required for the transmission model. We report variations up to 1400% in absorption coefficient for different pixels at the same water mass fraction of the overall system. This work provides a proof-of-concept demonstration of a cost-effective non-contact inline monitoring system based on THz technology for use in the ceramic production process.

title: Physics-Aware Semantic Graph Classifier for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Lingling Li, Jiadong Lin, Licheng Jiao, Fang Liu, Xu Liu, Yangyang Li, Wenping Ma
abstract: Remote sensing scenes face the challenges of high inter class similarity and high intra class diversity in classification. Methods based on deep learning have advanced the field by learning discriminative features, but these methods have two key limitations: 1) the scope of intra-image feature extraction is limited to the planar spatial domain, mainly capturing visual features such as color and shape, while neglecting other important physical information like the vertical structure of scene inference and its frequency domain features; and 2) the lack of inter-image relationship modeling, which prevents the capture of latent semantic correlations among different scene categories. To address these issues, we propose a physics-aware semantic graph classifier (PASG) for remote sensing image classification. Firstly, we propose a height-aware spatial-frequency feature extraction module (HASF), which represents key physical attributes by integrating the vertical structure, spatial features, and frequency domain information of scenes. Secondly, we design a semantic-aware global-local encoder (SGLE), modeling the dataset as a graph structure where the physical features of images serve as graph nodes. SGLE refines the representation of each node by capturing local neighborhood structures and global contextual dependencies, leading to a robust cross-image contextual semantic representation. Experiments on four publicly available remote sensing datasets (AID, UCM, WHU-RS19, and NWPU-RESISC45) achieve optimal overall accuracies of 98.60%, 99.81%, 99.42%, and 97.55%, respectively. These results outperform state-of-the-art methods and validate the effectiveness of the proposed PASG classifier.

title: A Root Prediction System for Single-Variable Equations with Existing Taylor Polynomials
authors: Seokjun Jeong, Yoosoo Oh
abstract: Root finding is a fundamental problem in numerical analysis with applications across scientific computing, control systems, and optimization. Classical iterative algorithms such as Newton-Raphson, bisection, and secant methods are highly sensitive to initial values and may fail to converge reliably for nonlinear equations, particularly when encountering zero derivatives, ill-conditioned Jacobians, or complex nonlinear structures. To address these limitations, we propose a direct root prediction framework that reformulates root finding as a structured prediction problem. The proposed system consists of four stages: (1) a Transformer-based interval predictor identifies the top-25 candidate intervals likely to contain real roots, (2) variable shifting and 25th-order Maclaurin expansion generate local polynomial representations, (3) coefficient-based neural models generate multiple candidate roots from Taylor coefficients, and (4) residual-based selection and validation identify the final numerically reliable root. Experiments on 10,000 test equations spanning 12 function families show that the proposed framework achieves strong performance under a strict residual threshold of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−10</sup>. Additional operating-point analysis indicates that using a 25th-order local expansion together with top-25 candidate intervals provides the most stable overall trade-off between success rate and computational cost across the evaluated model variants. Under the limited-prior-information evaluation setting adopted in this study, all proposed variants show competitive performance relative to the baseline numerical solver while producing validated residuals on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−12</sup> to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−11</sup> under the adopted evaluation protocol. Further analysis indicates that the observed performance ceiling is primarily governed by representation-level limitations of truncated Taylor expansions rather than by deficiencies of a specific prediction model.
